title: Jaccard Similarity
concept: Jaccard similarity measures the overlap of unique words between two texts, dividing the intersection size by the union size.

exercises.prompt: When finding the "unique" words in a sentence, how many times do we count a word that appears twice?
exercises.explain: Unique means we only count each word once, even if it appears multiple times in the text.

cards.title: Spotting Twin Sentences
cards.body: How do search engines know if two sentences mean almost the same thing, even if they aren't identical? They count the shared words using a clever mathematical recipe called Jaccard Similarity!

cards.title: What is Jaccard Similarity?
cards.body: Jaccard Similarity is a formula that measures how similar two sets of text are. It does this by dividing the number of unique words they share (intersection) by the total number of unique words across both texts combined (union).

cards.title: The Shared Suitcase
cards.body: Imagine you and a friend are packing for a trip. To find your shared tastes, you count how many identical items you both packed (intersection) and divide that by the total unique items you brought combined (union). If you both packed sunglasses and a towel, but brought 8 other different things, your overlap is small!

cards.title: Calculation in Action
cards.body:
Let's compare "cat naps" and "cat plays". 
• Shared words (intersection): ["cat"] -> Size 1.
• Total unique words (union): ["cat", "naps", "plays"] -> Size 3.
• Jaccard Similarity = 1 divided by 3, which is 0.33 (or 33% similarity).

cards.title: Repetition Doesn't Count!
cards.body: Some people think repeating a word over and over increases similarity. It doesn't! Jaccard similarity only cares about *unique* words. Repeating "cat" ten times in "cat naps" won't change the score because we only count "cat" once.

cards.title: Visualizing Overlap
cards.body: Jaccard similarity changes based on how much vocabulary overlap exists between two groups of words.

right.text: "Red Apple" vs "Sweet Red Apple" (Many words in common)

left.text: "Green Apple" vs "Red Cherry" (No words in common)

exercises.prompt: What is the range of values for Jaccard similarity?
exercises.explain: Jaccard similarity is always a number between 0 (no shared words at all) and 1 (all unique words are shared, meaning they are identical in vocabulary).

exercises.prompt: True or False: "Python is fun" and "Fun is Python" have a Jaccard similarity score of 1.0.
exercises.explain: Jaccard similarity only looks at the set of unique words. It does not look at the order of the words or grammar structure.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word in the Jaccard similarity formula.
exercises.explain: Jaccard similarity divides the size of the shared words (intersection) by the size of all unique words combined (union).

exercises.prompt: Two sentences are "I love dogs" and "Dogs love I". A computer calculated their Jaccard similarity. Which statement is the error?

options.text: The sentences have a similarity of 0.0
The similarity is less than 1.0 because word order changed

exercises.explain: Option C is incorrect because Jaccard similarity does not care about word order, only the presence of unique words. Since both sentences contain the exact same unique words, their Jaccard similarity is a perfect 1.0 (100%).

exercises.prompt: Let's test your Jaccard skills! What is the Jaccard similarity between the phrase "blue sky" and "red sky"?
exercises.explain: Jaccard similarity measures the exact overlap of unique elements divided by all unique elements combined. For "blue sky" and "red sky", the unique words are {blue, red, sky}. The intersection is {sky} (1 word). The union is {blue, red, sky} (3 words). 1 divided by 3 is approximately 0.33!
